coredata.dc:title: Crystallization kinetics of a bulk amorphous Cu–Ti–Zr–Ni alloy investigated by differential scanning calorimetry

Xing, D.W.
Shen, J.
Sun, J.F.
Wei, S.D.
He, H.J.
McCartney, D.G.
coredata.dc:description: The crystallization kinetics of a newly developed bulk amorphous Cu52.5Ti30Zr11.5Ni6 alloy was investigated by differential scanning calorimetry. Under continuous heating conditions, the activation energies for the glass transition and crystallization at the onset, first peak and second peak crystallization temperatures are determined by means of Kissinger plots to be 356.86, 296.52, 288.77 and 257.19kJ/mol, respectively. However, under isothermal conditions the average activation energy for crystallization is about 377.6kJ/mol obtained by using the Arrhenius equation, which is much larger than that deduced from Kissinger plots. The local Avrami exponent n(x) is mostly in the range 2.4–3.1, implying that the crystallization is mainly governed by diffusion-controlled, three-dimensional growth with nearly constant nucleation rate.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Bulk metallic glass
Crystallization kinetics
Activation energy
Avrami exponent

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD patterns taken from the cross-section of the as-cast 5mm (a) and 3mm (b) Cu52.5Ti30Zr11.5Ni6 alloys.
DSC curves of the bulk amorphous Cu52.5Ti30Zr11.5Ni6 alloy at different heating rates.
Kissinger plots of the bulk amorphous Cu52.5Ti30Zr11.5Ni6 alloy.
Isothermal DSC curves of the bulk amorphous Cu52.5Ti30Zr11.5Ni6 alloy.
Relationship between crystallized volume fraction and annealing time at different temperatures.
Activation energy E(x) vs. x deduced from Arrhenius equation.
JMA plots for the crystallization of the bulk amorphous Cu52.5Ti30Zr11.5Ni6 alloy at different annealing temperatures.

TEM images showing the morphologies of the quenched-in crystal (a) and the precipitated crystalline particles surrounding a quenched-in crystals (b) in the as-cast and isothermal annealed Zr56.6Cu17.3Ni12.5Al9.6Ti4 alloys, respectively [16].
Relationship between local Avrami exponent n(x) and crystallized volume fraction x at different annealing temperatures.
The characteristic temperatures T g, T x, T p1 and T p2 of bulk amorphous Cu52.5Ti30Zr11.5Ni6 alloy at different heating rates
The kinetic parameters of bulk amorphous Cu52.5Ti30Zr11.5Ni6 alloy isothermally annealed in the supercooled liquid region

article.ce:title: Crystallization kinetics of a bulk amorphous Cu–Ti–Zr–Ni alloy investigated by differential scanning calorimetry

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Harbin Institute of Technology, Harbin 150001, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Mechanical Engineering, Aviation University of Air Force, Changchun 130022, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Mechanical, Materials and Manufacturing Engineering, University of Nottingham, University Park, Nottingham NG7 2RD, UK

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The crystallization kinetics of a newly developed bulk amorphous Cu52.5Ti30Zr11.5Ni6 alloy was investigated by differential scanning calorimetry. Under continuous heating conditions, the activation energies for the glass transition and crystallization at the onset, first peak and second peak crystallization temperatures are determined by means of Kissinger plots to be 356.86, 296.52, 288.77 and 257.19kJ/mol, respectively. However, under isothermal conditions the average activation energy for crystallization is about 377.6kJ/mol obtained by using the Arrhenius equation, which is much larger than that deduced from Kissinger plots. The local Avrami exponent n(x) is mostly in the range 2.4–3.1, implying that the crystallization is mainly governed by diffusion-controlled, three-dimensional growth with nearly constant nucleation rate.

ce:section.ce:para: Bulk metallic glasses (BMGs) or bulk amorphous alloys (BAAs) are promising materials for special structural or functional use owing to specific superior properties compared to their crystalline counterparts [1,2]. In recent years, Inoue et al. have succeeded in fabricating new Cu-based BMGs in Cu-(Zr,Hf)-Ti [3] and Cu-(Zr,Hf)-Al [4,5] systems containing more than 50at.% Cu. Cu-based BMGs possess many potential advantages over other amorphous alloys, including higher fracture strength and elastic moduli, and lower materials cost, etc. A great deal of effort has been devoted to exploring new Cu-based BMGs. In particular, subsequent study has demonstrated Cu-(Zr,Hf)-Ti BMGs consist of a glassy phase and nanocrystalline particles with a size range of 3–5nm [6,7]. The nanocrystalline particles make a significant contribution to the good mechanical properties of Cu-based bulk amorphous alloys. Thus, it is necessary to understand the thermal stability and microstructural evolution for gaining a controlled crystallization. The investigation of crystallization kinetics is an important approach to assessing the thermal stability of amorphous alloys. However, too limited information is available to date for understanding the crystallization kinetics of Cu-based BMGs.

Recently, the present authors have developed a new Cu52.5Ti30Zr11.5Ni6 amorphous alloy with a good glass forming ability (GFA). It can be quenched into glassy samples up to 5mm in diameter by the copper mold casting method, and exhibits high fracture strength exceeding 2180MPa under uniaxial compressive conditions. In this study, the thermal stability and crystallization kinetics of the Cu52.5Ti30Zr11.5Ni6 alloy are investigated and the underlying mechanisms are discussed.

The alloy ingot with a nominal composition of Cu52.5Ti30Zr11.5Ni6 (at.%) was prepared by arc melting mixtures of pure Cu, Ti, Zr and Ni with a purity of better than 99.9% in a Ti-gettered argon atmosphere. The ingot was re-melted three times to ensure chemical homogeneity. Bulk cylindrical samples with diameters of 3 or 5mm and length of 40mm were prepared by re-melting the prealloyed ingot on the top of a copper mold, and then casting the molten liquid into the mold. The amorphicity of the as-cast sample was confirmed by X-ray diffractometry (XRD) using Cu Kα radiation. Thermal analysis was conducted using differential scanning calorimetry (Perkin-Elmer, DSC7) under a flowing argon atmosphere.

The as-cast Cu52.5Ti30Zr11.5Ni6 samples were determined, by XRD, to be fully amorphous, as shown in Fig. 1 . The broadened halo peak is the typical characteristic of an amorphous structure. Fig. 2 shows the DSC curves of Cu52.5Ti30Zr11.5Ni6 BMG performed at different heating rates. The heating rates were chosen as 10, 20, 40, 60 and 80K/min. As can be seen from Fig. 2, there exists three pronounced exothermic peaks, corresponding to the multiple-stage crystallization mode. The apparent activation energy for glass transition or crystallization of an amorphous alloy under continuous heating conditions is often estimated by the Kissinger equation [8]:
(1)
ln
ϕ
T 2
= −
E
R T
+ constant
where ϕ is the heating rate; E the apparent activation energy for the process; R the gas constant; T is the specific temperature, such as glass transition temperature, T g, onset temperature, T x, or peak temperature, T p, of crystallization. By plotting ln(ϕ/T 2) versus 1/(RT), an approximately straight line with a slope of E g, E x or E p can be obtained, in which E is the apparent activation energy of the whole reaction. It has been suggested that the Kissinger method is valid for determining the glass transition activation energy [9]. In the present work, the glass transition temperature, T g, the onset crystallization temperature, T x, the first and second peak temperatures, T p1 and T p2, respectively, of crystallization were chosen as the specific temperatures to evaluate the activation energies of the amorphous Cu52.5Ti30Zr11.5Ni6 alloy. Table 1 summarizes the characteristic temperatures of the Cu52.5Ti30Zr11.5Ni6 alloy at different heating rates. The Kissinger plots are shown in Fig. 3 . The activation energies of E g, E x, E p1 and E p2 are deduced to be 356.86±18.04, 296.52±8.34, 288.77±13.75 and 257.19±6.43kJ/mol, respectively.

The isothermal kinetics of Cu52.5Ti30Zr11.5Ni6 BMG was characterized by annealing the samples at different temperatures ranging from 710 to 738K. The DSC traces are shown in Fig. 4 . All the DSC traces exhibit single exothermic peaks after a certain incubation period. The crystallized volume fraction, x, as a function of annealing time, can be obtained by integrating the area underneath the DSC curve. Fig. 5 presents the relationship between the crystallized volume fraction and annealing time. The plots obtained display a sigmoidal shape. It can be seen that the incubation time for crystallization becomes longer and the crystallization process becomes slower as the annealing temperature decreases. In the case of isothermal annealing, the Arrhenius equation [10] is often used to evaluate the activation energy for crystallization of an amorphous alloy, i.e.,
(2)
t ( x ) =
t 0
exp
E ( x )
R T
where t 0 is a constant, E(x) the activation energy for crystallization with the transformed volume fraction x and R is the gas constant. The plot of ln t(x) versus 1/(RT) at a given crystallized volume fraction x yields a nearly straight line with a slope of E(x). Fig. 6 shows the relationship between the activation energy of amorphous Cu52.5Ti30Zr11.5Ni6 alloy and the crystallized volume fraction, x. It is seen that at the initial crystallization stage, the activation energy for crystallization of amorphous Cu52.5Ti30Zr11.5Ni6 alloy decreases rapidly from 375 to 365kJ/mol up to x =6%, which is very close to E g obtained from Kissinger equation (358kJ/mol). The decreasing trend means that the crystallization process proceeds more easily at the initial stage. Then, the activation energy starts to increase steadily and remains almost constant when the crystallized volume fraction falls in the range of 50–90%, implying that the subsequent crystallization process becomes sluggish as the phase transformation proceeds. It is worth noting that the average activation energy obtained by using Arrhenius equation (about 377.6kJ/mol) is much larger than E x or E p1 obtained by using Kissinger equation. Wei et al. [11] have also observed this phenomenon and indicated the activation energy derived from either Kissinger or Arrhenius plots might have temperature dependence. Compared with isothermal annealing, the sample subjected to continuous heating condition would crystallize at a relatively higher temperature. Crystallization at higher temperatures above T x makes it easier to transform from the amorphous state to crystalline phases.

The isothermal annealing time dependence of the crystallized volume fraction, x, can be modeled by the Johnson–Mehl–Avrami (JMA) equation as following [12]:
(3)
x ( t ) = 1 − exp [ − k
( t − τ )
n
]
where x(t) is the crystallized volume fraction, t the annealing time, τ the incubation time for nucleation (defined as the time scale between the time t 0 to t 0.5%, t 0 and t 0.5% are the times to reach the annealing temperature and 0.5% crystallized volume fraction, respectively), k the reaction rate constant, R the gas constant and n is the Avrami exponent which is related to the underlying crystallization mechanisms. The JMA plot of ln[−ln(1− x)] versus ln(t
−
τ) gives a nearly straight line with the slope of n, as shown in Fig. 7 . Table 2 presents the kinetic parameters of the Cu52.5Ti30Zr11.5Ni6 BMG isothermally annealed in the supercooled liquid region. As can be seen from Table 2, the Avrami exponents vary from 2.42 to 2.64 for different annealing temperatures. The average Avrami exponent of about 2.5 implies a mainly diffusion-controlled, three-dimensional growth during the crystallization of the bulk amorphous Cu52.5Ti30Zr11.5Ni6 alloy [13]. The relatively small values of the incubation and crystallization times (as shown in Table 2) suggest that crystallization initiates from the growth of quenched-in nuclei [14,15]. The assumption is consistent with the observations that nanocrystals of 3–5nm in size can be detected in the amorphous matrix of Cu-based BMGs [6,7]. In our previous study on the crystallization behavior of a Zr-based BMG [16], we found that the quenched-in crystalline phase indeed provided heterogeneous nucleation sites for the precipitation of primary phases during the crystallization process, as shown in Fig. 8 . The investigation on the microstructural evolution of the amorphous Cu52.5Ti30Zr11.5Ni6 alloy during crystallization is in progress.

It is widely accepted that the nucleation and growth rates of crystals do not remain constant during the whole crystallization process of an amorphous alloy. Therefore, the so-called local Avrami exponent is introduced and defined as [10]:
(4)
n ( x ) =
∂ ⁡ ln [ − ln ( 1 − x ) ]
∂ ⁡ ln ( t − τ )
Fig. 9 shows the relationship between the local Avrami exponent n(x) and the crystallized volume fraction x corresponding to five different annealing temperatures. By referring to the fact that n(x)≤2.0 when x(t) is below 4%, we may draw the conclusion that at the early stage the crystallization process is mainly governed by one-dimensional growth with a decreasing nucleation rate [13], and enormous number of short-range ordering clusters act as overcritical nuclei in the supercooled liquid region. The growth of the overcritical nuclei and formation of new nuclei require simultaneous rearrangement of different species of atoms. Due to the dense randomly packed atomic configuration of multi-component amorphous alloy, diffusion of atoms is difficult, which retards the nucleation and growth, leading to a temporarily decreasing nucleation rate. As the crystallization proceeds, n(x) starts to increase and falls mostly in the range 2.4–3.1 until x(t) reaches 90%, revealing crystallization is mainly controlled by three-dimensional growth with nearly constant nucleation rate. The growth of the initial nuclei changes the composition of the matrix surrounding the nuclei. The compositional fluctuation facilitates the nucleation adjacent to the growing nuclei, leading to a higher but nearly constant nucleation rate compared to the nucleation rate at the initial crystallization stage.

The crystallization kinetics of a bulk amorphous Cu52.5Ti30Zr11.5Ni6 alloy was characterized by DSC under isothermal and non-isothermal conditions. The average activation energy derived from the Arrhenius equation is much larger than that obtained from the Kissinger plot. The different temperatures at which crystallization occurs are responsible for the discrepancy in the activation energy. Under isothermal annealing, the initial crystallization stage when x(t)≤4% is governed by one-dimensional growth with a decreasing nucleation rate. As crystallization proceeds, the crystallization mode is mainly controlled by three-dimensional growth with nearly constant nucleation rate. The relatively small values of the incubation and crystallization times suggest the heterogeneous nuclei primarily originate from the quenched-in short-range ordering clusters.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: The Theory of Transformation in Metals and Alloys

ce:other-ref.ce:textref: D.W. Xing, Ph.D. Thesis, School of Materials Science and Engineering, Harbin Institute of Technology, Harbin, China, 2003.
